Q3: Multi-factor authentication was not available for any of Le Bonbon Croissant’s core services. Threat actors can use credentials to access services across the network without any further authentication if credentials were compromised during an assault. MIDDLE-EAST-7 advises that business-critical services use some type of Multi- Factor Authentication right away.
Q5: Changing local Administrator passwords in Windows workstations and securely handle sensitive data such as passwords and SSNs across Le Bonbon Croissant systems are our two  most essential short-term recommendations. While this is not an easy step to make, it is critical for Le Bonbon Croissant's long-term survival. The following are our additional short-term recommendations: On Windows workstations, change local Administrator passwords to longer, more challenging passwords that aren't reused. Using hashing and encryption methods, securely manage personal information such as SSNs and passwords in databases and other services. Unauthenticated access to services like PostgreSQL should be disabled.
Q6: During the examination, some solid security controls were uncovered. These precautions should be monitored on a regular basis in order to keep Le Bonbon Croissant safe. The following conclusions have been made about posture: Password Authentication for SSH is enabled: During this examination, no SSH instances that supported password authentication were found. Le Bonbon Croissant has effectively eliminated password brute forcing as a danger to SSH access systems by adding this control.

Under the assumption that this host was a PLC, we planned our operation to carefully avoid sending unnecessary traffic to this host. We were successful in directing our attacks to other hosts and scrutinizing details about this host and its exposure without compromising the operational integrity of LBC.

Q7: We had access to the TCP serial connection and were able to run queries and dump memory from the PLC. We wanted to restrain our operations on it to reading it, exclusively, but if we were to access the system's source code we could perform more testing offline. Q9: After reviewing our initial options and reviewing our scans of the environment, we selected to stick with our plan of not sending active attacks to the PLC host.
